question:
During an inter-school football coaching clinic, two players strike identical footballs with the same initial speed of 20 m s⁻¹ but at different angles to the horizontal. Player P kicks the ball at 30°, while Player Q kicks it at 60°.

Ignoring air resistance, which statement correctly compares the horizontal components of their launch velocities?

options.A: Player P has the greater horizontal component of velocity.
options.B: Player Q has the greater horizontal component of velocity.
options.C: Both players have equal horizontal components of velocity.
options.D: The horizontal components cannot be determined without knowing the masses of the footballs.

answer_text: Player P has the greater horizontal component of velocity.

explanation.question_restate: This question compares the horizontal components of the launch velocities of two footballs kicked with the same speed but at different angles. You are required to determine which football has the larger horizontal component.
explanation.principle:
Projectile motion consists of two independent motions: horizontal motion and vertical motion.

The horizontal component of the launch velocity is obtained using:

vₓ = u cos θ

where:

• vₓ = horizontal component of velocity
• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection

For the same launch speed, the projectile launched at the smaller angle has the larger value of cos θ and therefore the larger horizontal component of velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Write the given information.

Launch speed, u = 20 m s⁻¹.

Player P: θ = 30°.

Player Q: θ = 60°.

Step 2: Determine the horizontal component for Player P.

vₓ = 20 × cos 30°

= 20 × 0.866

≈ 17.3 m s⁻¹.

Step 3: Determine the horizontal component for Player Q.

vₓ = 20 × cos 60°

= 20 × 0.5

= 10.0 m s⁻¹.

Step 4: Compare the two values.

17.3 m s⁻¹ is greater than 10.0 m s⁻¹.

Therefore, Player P has the greater horizontal component of velocity.

Step 5: Select the correct option.

Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because increasing the launch angle reduces the horizontal component when the launch speed remains the same.
Option C is incorrect because cos 30° and cos 60° are not equal, so the horizontal components are different.
Option D is incorrect because the mass of the football does not affect the calculation of the horizontal component of launch velocity.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever two projectiles are launched with the same speed, compare their horizontal components using u cos θ. A smaller launch angle gives a larger horizontal component, while a larger launch angle gives a smaller horizontal component.
explanation.final_answer: A. Player P has the greater horizontal component of velocity.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine pushing a trolley partly forward and partly upward. The more your push is directed forward, the greater the forward motion. Since Player P kicks at the smaller angle, more of the launch speed acts horizontally, giving a larger horizontal component.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion (Fundamentals) → Resolving Launch Velocity into Horizontal and Vertical Components for detailed explanations, worked examples, and additional JAMB-style practice on velocity components.

question: During a basketball training session, two players release identical balls with the same launch speed of 28 m s⁻¹. Player P throws the ball at an angle of 35° to the horizontal, while Player Q throws the ball at an angle of 55° to the horizontal. Neglecting air resistance, which player gives the ball the greater initial vertical component of velocity?

options.A: Player P, because the smaller launch angle produces a larger vertical component
options.B: Player Q, because the larger launch angle produces a larger vertical component
options.C: Both players, because the launch speeds are the same
options.D: Neither player, because the vertical component depends only on gravity

answer_text: Player Q, because the larger launch angle produces a larger vertical component

explanation.question_restate: This question compares the initial vertical components of velocity for two projectiles launched with the same speed but at different angles.
explanation.principle:
When a projectile is launched at an angle to the horizontal, its velocity is resolved into two independent components.

The horizontal component is given by:

vₓ = u cos θ

The vertical component is given by:

vᵧ = u sin θ

where:

• u is the launch speed.
• θ is the angle of projection.
• vᵧ is the initial vertical component.

For projectiles launched with the same speed, the one with the larger value of sin θ has the greater vertical component of velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Launch speed, u = 28 m s⁻¹ for both players.

Player P: θ = 35°.

Player Q: θ = 55°.

Step 2: Compare the sine values.

sin 35° ≈ 0.574.

sin 55° ≈ 0.819.

Since sin 55° is greater than sin 35°, Player Q has the larger vertical component.

Step 3: Verify by calculation.

Player P:

vᵧ = 28 × 0.574 ≈ 16.1 m s⁻¹.

Player Q:

vᵧ = 28 × 0.819 ≈ 22.9 m s⁻¹.

Step 4: Compare the results.

22.9 m s⁻¹ > 16.1 m s⁻¹.

Therefore, Player Q gives the ball the greater initial vertical component of velocity.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because a smaller launch angle gives a smaller value of sin θ and therefore a smaller vertical component when the launch speeds are equal.
Option C is incorrect because equal launch speeds do not guarantee equal vertical components. The launch angle must also be considered.
Option D is incorrect because gravity affects the motion after release. The initial vertical component is determined by the launch speed and launch angle.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever two projectiles have the same launch speed, compare their launch angles.

• Larger angle → larger vertical component (u sin θ).
• Smaller angle → larger horizontal component (u cos θ).

Do not confuse the effect of gravity after launch with the initial components of velocity.
explanation.final_answer: B. Player Q, because the larger launch angle produces a larger vertical component
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine splitting the launch speed into two parts: one horizontal and one vertical. As the launch angle increases, more of the speed is directed upward. Since 55° is greater than 35°, Player Q's throw starts with a greater upward speed.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals → Resolving Velocity into Horizontal and Vertical Components for detailed explanations, worked examples, and JAMB-style practice questions on projectile motion.

question: A public park has a decorative water fountain whose nozzle can be adjusted to different angles while the speed of water leaving the nozzle remains constant. Which statement best describes the effect of increasing the launch angle of the water jet from 25° to 55°?

options.A: The horizontal component of the launch velocity increases while the vertical component decreases.
options.B: Both the horizontal and vertical components of the launch velocity increase.
options.C: The horizontal component of the launch velocity decreases while the vertical component increases.
options.D: Both the horizontal and vertical components of the launch velocity remain unchanged.

answer_text: The horizontal component of the launch velocity decreases while the vertical component increases.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of how changing the angle of projection affects the horizontal and vertical components of a projectile's launch velocity when the launch speed remains constant.
explanation.principle:
Projectile motion consists of two independent motions occurring simultaneously.

The launch velocity can be resolved into:

• Horizontal component: vx = u cos θ
• Vertical component: vy = u sin θ

where:

• u is the launch speed.
• θ is the launch angle.
• vx is the horizontal component.
• vy is the vertical component.

As the launch angle increases while the launch speed remains constant:

• cos θ decreases, so the horizontal component decreases.
• sin θ increases (up to 90°), so the vertical component increases.

Thus, changing only the launch angle redistributes the same launch speed between the horizontal and vertical directions.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what remains constant.

The speed of water leaving the nozzle does not change. Only the launch angle changes.

Step 2: Consider the horizontal component.

Since vx = u cos θ, increasing θ from 25° to 55° reduces the value of cos θ. Therefore, the horizontal component becomes smaller.

Step 3: Consider the vertical component.

Since vy = u sin θ, increasing θ from 25° to 55° increases the value of sin θ. Therefore, the vertical component becomes larger.

Step 4: Compare the options.

Only Option C correctly states that the horizontal component decreases while the vertical component increases.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it reverses the actual effect of increasing the launch angle.
Option B is incorrect because the two velocity components cannot both increase when the launch speed remains constant.
Option D is incorrect because changing the launch angle changes how the launch velocity is divided into horizontal and vertical components.
explanation.exam_tip:
When the launch speed is constant, remember:

• Larger launch angle → smaller horizontal component.
• Larger launch angle → larger vertical component.

Do not confuse the total launch speed with its components. The launch speed remains the same, but its distribution changes with angle.
explanation.final_answer: C. The horizontal component of the launch velocity decreases while the vertical component increases.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pointing a garden hose more upward without changing how fast the water leaves the nozzle.

More of the water's speed is directed upward, while less is directed forward. This makes the vertical component increase and the horizontal component decrease.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals → Velocity Components and Effect of Launch Angle for detailed explanations, worked examples, and additional JAMB-style practice questions in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question: Engineers are testing an automated package launcher in a manufacturing plant. A package leaves the launcher horizontally and lands on a conveyor belt moving at a constant speed. If the package maintains a constant horizontal speed of 8 m s⁻¹ for 6 s before landing, what horizontal distance does it cover?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding that, in ideal projectile motion, the horizontal component of velocity remains constant. You are required to determine the horizontal distance travelled by multiplying the constant horizontal speed by the time of motion.
explanation.principle:
Projectile motion consists of two independent motions occurring simultaneously.

The horizontal motion is uniform because no horizontal force acts on the projectile after it is launched, provided air resistance is neglected.

The horizontal distance travelled is therefore calculated using:

x = vₓt

where:

• x = horizontal distance travelled
• vₓ = constant horizontal velocity
• t = time of motion

A common beginner's question is why gravity is not included in this calculation. Gravity acts only in the vertical direction, so it does not change the horizontal speed.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Horizontal speed, vₓ = 8 m s⁻¹

Time, t = 6 s

Step 2: Write the appropriate equation.

x = vₓt

Step 3: Substitute the values.

x = 8 × 6

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

x = 48 m

Step 5: Compare the result with the options.

48 m corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (14 m) is incorrect because it does not result from multiplying the given speed by the given time.
Option B (36 m) is incorrect because it represents an incorrect calculation and underestimates the horizontal distance.
Option D (56 m) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect multiplication or the use of an incorrect value for time or speed.
explanation.exam_tip: For horizontal motion in projectile problems, always remember that the horizontal velocity remains constant if air resistance is neglected. Whenever horizontal speed and time are given, use x = vₓt before considering the vertical motion separately.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine sliding a parcel smoothly across a moving belt. If it moves at a constant speed of 8 m every second for 6 seconds, it travels:

8 × 6 = 48 m.

Since gravity does not change the horizontal speed, the package covers 48 m horizontally.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals → Constant Horizontal Velocity for detailed lessons on independent horizontal and vertical motions, worked examples, and additional JAMB-style practice questions in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

subtopic: Projectile Motion (Fundamentals) - Vertical velocity under gravity
question: During a Physics laboratory demonstration, a small ball is projected vertically upward with an initial vertical velocity of 16 m s⁻¹. Neglecting air resistance and taking the acceleration due to gravity as 10 m s⁻², what will be the vertical velocity of the ball after 1.2 s?

explanation.question_restate: The question requires you to determine the vertical velocity of a projectile after it has been moving upward for a given time under the influence of gravity.
explanation.principle:
Projectile motion consists of independent horizontal and vertical motions. In the vertical direction, gravity produces a constant downward acceleration.

For vertical motion, the equation

v = u + at

is used, where:

• v = final vertical velocity
• u = initial vertical velocity
• a = vertical acceleration due to gravity
• t = time taken

Since gravity acts downward, upward motion is taken as positive and the acceleration becomes -g.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Initial vertical velocity, u = 16 m s⁻¹

Acceleration, a = -10 m s⁻²

Time, t = 1.2 s

Step 2: Apply the first equation of motion.

v = u + at

Step 3: Substitute the values.

v = 16 + (-10 × 1.2)

v = 16 - 12

Step 4: Calculate the result.

v = 4 m s⁻¹

Step 5: Interpret the answer.

The velocity is still positive, meaning the ball is still moving upward, although it has slowed down because of gravity.

Therefore, the correct answer is 4 m s⁻¹ upward.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it results from an incorrect subtraction or misuse of the acceleration.
Option C is incorrect because it assumes gravity has reduced the velocity by only 4 m s⁻¹ instead of 12 m s⁻¹.
Option D is incorrect because although gravity acts downward, the ball has not yet reversed its direction after 1.2 s. Its velocity is still upward.
explanation.exam_tip: When solving vertical motion problems, always assign the correct sign to gravity. If upward is positive, then the acceleration due to gravity is negative. A positive final velocity means the object is still rising, while a negative final velocity means it is descending.
explanation.simple_explanation: Throw a ball straight upward. Gravity continually slows it down. After 1.2 s, its upward speed has reduced from 16 m s⁻¹ to 4 m s⁻¹, but it has not yet started falling. Therefore, its velocity is still upward.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals → Vertical Motion Under Gravity for detailed explanations of vertical velocity, acceleration due to gravity, sign convention, worked examples, and additional JAMB-style practice questions.

question: Visitors at a recreational park in Lagos watch a ball being projected from level ground with a speed of 22 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 45° to the horizontal. Neglecting air resistance and take g = 10 m s⁻². What is the horizontal distance travelled by the ball after 2 s of flight?

explanation.question_restate: The question asks for the horizontal distance covered by a projectile 2 seconds after it is launched at 22 m s⁻¹ and 45° above the horizontal. Since air resistance is neglected, the horizontal component of velocity remains constant throughout the motion.
explanation.principle:
Projectile motion consists of two independent motions occurring simultaneously.

The horizontal motion is uniform because no horizontal force acts on the projectile.

The horizontal distance travelled is given by:

x = vₓt

where:

• x = horizontal distance
• vₓ = horizontal component of the launch velocity
• t = time of flight considered

The horizontal component of the launch velocity is obtained from:

vₓ = u cosθ

where:

• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection.
steps:
Step 1: Write the known values.

Launch speed, u = 22 m s⁻¹
Angle of projection, θ = 45°
Time, t = 2 s

Step 2: Determine the horizontal component of the velocity.

vₓ = u cos45°

= 22 × 0.707

≈ 15.56 m s⁻¹

Step 3: Calculate the horizontal distance.

x = vₓt

= 15.56 × 2

= 31.12 m

Step 4: Round the answer appropriately.

Horizontal distance ≈ 31.1 m.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

Option B corresponds to 31.1 m and is therefore correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (22.0 m) incorrectly assumes the horizontal distance is equal to the launch speed multiplied by one second or ignores the velocity component.
Option C (44.0 m) incorrectly uses the full launch speed instead of resolving it into its horizontal component.
Option D (15.6 m) represents approximately the horizontal component of velocity in m s⁻¹, not the horizontal distance travelled after 2 s.
explanation.exam_tip: For horizontal motion in projectile problems, always resolve the launch speed into its horizontal component first. Gravity does not change the horizontal velocity, so use x = vₓt after finding vₓ = u cosθ.
explanation.simple_explanation: Think of the ball as moving in two directions at the same time. Gravity changes only the up-and-down motion, while the sideways motion continues at a constant speed. First find the sideways speed using u cos45°, then multiply it by the time to obtain the horizontal distance.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals for lessons on resolving launch velocity into horizontal and vertical components, the independence of projectile motion, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided practice.

question:
During a WAEC Physics practical lesson, four candidates discussed the motion of a small steel ball after it left the end of a smooth horizontal track.

Candidate I: The horizontal speed of the ball gradually decreases because gravity acts in the direction of motion.

Candidate II: A heavier steel ball would follow the same path as a lighter one if both are released with the same horizontal speed and air resistance is neglected.

Candidate III: Once the ball leaves the track, no force acts on it because it has already been projected.

Candidate IV: The horizontal and vertical motions of the ball are independent of each other.

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to identify common misconceptions about projectile motion by evaluating four statements made during a practical experiment involving a horizontally projected steel ball.
explanation.principle:
A projectile is any object that, after being launched, moves under the influence of gravity alone when air resistance is neglected.

Projectile motion consists of two independent motions occurring simultaneously:

• Horizontal motion, in which the horizontal velocity remains constant because no horizontal force acts on the projectile.

• Vertical motion, in which gravity produces a constant downward acceleration of approximately 9.8 m s⁻² (often taken as 10 m s⁻² in JAMB calculations).

The mass of the projectile does not affect its motion in ideal projectile motion because all objects experience the same gravitational acceleration in the absence of air resistance.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Candidate I's statement.

This statement is incorrect. Gravity acts vertically downward, not horizontally. Therefore, it does not reduce the horizontal speed of the projectile in ideal motion.

Step 2: Examine Candidate II's statement.

This statement is correct. If air resistance is neglected, projectiles of different masses released with the same initial horizontal speed follow the same trajectory because gravity accelerates them equally.

Step 3: Examine Candidate III's statement.

This statement is incorrect. After projection, gravity continues to act on the ball throughout its flight. Although the supporting force from the track disappears, the gravitational force remains.

Step 4: Examine Candidate IV's statement.

This statement is correct. The horizontal and vertical components of projectile motion are independent. Gravity affects only the vertical component.

Step 5: Identify the correct combination.

Only Candidates II and IV made correct statements.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because Candidate I wrongly assumes that gravity acts in the horizontal direction.
Option C is incorrect because Candidate III incorrectly states that no force acts on the projectile after it leaves the track. Gravity continues to act.
Option D is incorrect because both Candidate I and Candidate III made incorrect statements.
explanation.exam_tip:
When solving projectile motion questions, remember these key facts:

• Gravity always acts vertically downward.
• Horizontal velocity remains constant when air resistance is neglected.
• The mass of a projectile does not affect its motion under ideal conditions.
• Horizontal and vertical motions occur independently but simultaneously.

Many JAMB questions are designed around these common misconceptions rather than lengthy calculations.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine rolling two steel balls of different masses off the same table at the same speed. Both move forward while falling downward together because gravity acts equally on both. Their horizontal motion is unaffected by gravity, and gravity continues to pull them downward throughout the flight.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals → Common Misconceptions to revise the forces acting on projectiles, independence of horizontal and vertical motion, and frequently tested JAMB conceptual questions.

question: Engineers testing an automated airport baggage handling system observe that a suitcase leaves the end of a horizontal conveyor belt and lands in a collection trolley. Assuming air resistance is negligible, which statement best explains the motion of the suitcase while it is in the air?

options.A: Its horizontal speed decreases continuously because gravity acts in the direction of motion.
options.B: Its horizontal and vertical motions are independent, with gravity affecting only the vertical motion.
options.C: Its vertical speed remains constant while its horizontal speed increases due to gravity.
options.D: Both its horizontal and vertical motions are accelerated equally by gravity.

answer_text: Its horizontal and vertical motions are independent, with gravity affecting only the vertical motion.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your understanding of simultaneous motions in projectile motion. It asks you to identify how the horizontal and vertical components of a projectile's motion behave after the object leaves a horizontal conveyor belt.
explanation.principle:
Projectile motion consists of two independent motions occurring at the same time.

The horizontal motion is uniform because no horizontal force acts on the projectile when air resistance is neglected.

The vertical motion is uniformly accelerated because gravity acts vertically downward with an acceleration of approximately 9.8 m s⁻² (often taken as 10 m s⁻² in JAMB calculations).

Since gravity acts only vertically, it changes the vertical velocity but does not affect the horizontal velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the instant the suitcase leaves the conveyor belt.

At this moment, the supporting force from the conveyor belt is removed, leaving gravity as the only significant force acting on the suitcase.

Step 2: Analyse the horizontal motion.

Since there is no horizontal force acting on the suitcase (neglecting air resistance), its horizontal velocity remains constant throughout its flight.

Step 3: Analyse the vertical motion.

Gravity causes the suitcase to accelerate downward, so its vertical velocity changes continuously during the motion.

Step 4: Evaluate the options.

Only Option B correctly states that the horizontal and vertical motions are independent and that gravity affects only the vertical motion.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because gravity acts vertically downward, not horizontally. Therefore, it does not reduce the horizontal speed.
Option C is incorrect because gravity changes the vertical speed continuously, while the horizontal speed remains constant.
Option D is incorrect because gravity does not accelerate the projectile horizontally. Only the vertical component experiences gravitational acceleration.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever you encounter projectile motion, separate the motion into horizontal and vertical components.

Remember these key facts:

• Horizontal velocity remains constant.
• Vertical motion is accelerated by gravity.
• The two motions occur simultaneously but are independent of each other.

Many JAMB questions test this principle rather than lengthy calculations.
explanation.final_answer: B. Its horizontal and vertical motions are independent, with gravity affecting only the vertical motion.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine rolling a ball off the edge of a table.

The ball continues moving forward at nearly the same speed while gravity pulls it downward at the same time.

The forward motion and the downward motion happen together but do not interfere with each other. This is why the horizontal and vertical motions are said to be independent.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals → Simultaneous Motions to learn why horizontal and vertical motions are independent, how gravity affects projectiles, and how to solve JAMB-style conceptual and numerical questions.

question: During a training exercise at a sports academy, two athletes throw identical balls simultaneously from the same point. Athlete P throws the ball horizontally with a speed of 14 m s⁻¹, while Athlete Q throws the ball horizontally with a speed of 20 m s⁻¹. Neglecting air resistance, which statement correctly compares the motions of the two balls?

options.A: Ball Q reaches the ground first because it has the greater horizontal speed.
options.B: Ball P reaches the ground first because its horizontal speed is smaller.
options.C: Both balls reach the ground at the same time, but Ball Q lands farther from the launch point.
options.D: Both balls travel the same horizontal distance because gravity acts equally on them.

answer_text: Both balls reach the ground at the same time, but Ball Q lands farther from the launch point.

explanation.question_restate: This question compares the motions of two projectiles launched horizontally at different speeds from the same height. It tests your understanding of the independence of horizontal and vertical motions.
explanation.principle:
Projectile motion consists of two independent motions occurring simultaneously.

The horizontal motion takes place with constant velocity because no horizontal force acts on the projectile when air resistance is neglected.

The vertical motion is uniformly accelerated by gravity.

Since both balls start from the same height with zero initial vertical velocity, gravity affects both in exactly the same way. Therefore, they remain in the air for the same duration. However, the ball with the greater horizontal speed covers a larger horizontal distance before landing.
steps:
Step 1: Compare the vertical motions.

Both balls are released horizontally from the same height. Their initial vertical velocities are zero, and both experience the same gravitational acceleration g.

Hence, both remain in the air for the same time.

Step 2: Compare the horizontal motions.

Horizontal distance is given by:

x = vₓt

Since both projectiles have the same time of flight, the one with the larger horizontal speed travels farther.

Step 3: Compare the given speeds.

Ball P: 14 m s⁻¹

Ball Q: 20 m s⁻¹

Because 20 m s⁻¹ > 14 m s⁻¹, Ball Q covers a greater horizontal distance before hitting the ground.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Both balls land at the same time, but Ball Q lands farther from the launch point.

Therefore, Option C is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because horizontal speed does not determine the time taken to reach the ground.
Option B is incorrect because a smaller horizontal speed does not reduce the time of flight.
Option D is incorrect because although gravity affects both equally, their horizontal speeds are different, so they do not travel the same horizontal distance.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB examinations, remember that gravity affects only the vertical component of projectile motion. If two projectiles are launched horizontally from the same height, they reach the ground simultaneously regardless of their horizontal speeds. The faster horizontal speed only increases the horizontal distance travelled.
explanation.final_answer: C. Both balls reach the ground at the same time, but Ball Q lands farther from the launch point.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine dropping two stones from the same balcony while giving one of them a stronger horizontal push. They both hit the ground together because gravity pulls them downward equally. However, the stone pushed faster lands farther away.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals → Independence of Horizontal and Vertical Motion for detailed explanations, worked examples and additional JAMB-style practice questions.

question: Engineers at a construction site use a mechanical launcher to transfer small bags of cement from the ground to a raised platform. After leaving the launcher, the bag travels through the air before landing on the platform. Neglecting air resistance, which statement best predicts the path followed by the bag?

options.A: It travels in a straight horizontal line because its horizontal speed remains constant.
options.B: It follows a parabolic path because it has uniform horizontal motion and vertically accelerated motion under gravity.
options.C: It moves vertically upward and then falls straight down because gravity acts only after it reaches its highest point.
options.D: It follows a circular path because gravity continuously changes its direction of motion.

answer_text: It follows a parabolic path because it has uniform horizontal motion and vertically accelerated motion under gravity.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to predict the trajectory of a projectile in a practical engineering situation by applying the fundamental principles of projectile motion.
explanation.principle:
A projectile is any object that is launched into the air and thereafter moves under the influence of gravity alone, provided air resistance is neglected.

Projectile motion consists of two independent motions occurring at the same time:

• Horizontal motion, which has constant velocity because no horizontal force acts on the projectile.

• Vertical motion, which is uniformly accelerated downward due to gravity.

The combination of these two independent motions produces a parabolic trajectory.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the forces acting after the bag leaves the launcher.

Once released, gravity is the only significant force acting on the bag because air resistance is neglected.

Step 2: Analyse the horizontal motion.

Since there is no horizontal force, the horizontal component of velocity remains constant throughout the flight.

Step 3: Analyse the vertical motion.

Gravity continuously accelerates the bag downward with acceleration g, causing its vertical velocity to change during the motion.

Step 4: Combine both motions.

Constant horizontal motion together with uniformly accelerated vertical motion produces a parabolic path.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

Only Option B correctly describes both the nature of the horizontal and vertical motions as well as the resulting trajectory.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because although the horizontal velocity remains constant, gravity changes the vertical motion. The overall path is therefore not a straight horizontal line.
Option C is incorrect because gravity acts on the projectile immediately after it is launched, not only after it reaches its highest point.
Option D is incorrect because a circular path requires a continuous centripetal force directed toward the centre of the circle. A projectile under gravity alone does not move in a circular path.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB asks about the path of a projectile, remember that the horizontal and vertical motions are independent.

Constant horizontal velocity combined with downward acceleration due to gravity always produces a parabolic trajectory when air resistance is neglected.
explanation.final_answer: B. It follows a parabolic path because it has uniform horizontal motion and vertically accelerated motion under gravity.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine throwing a ball across a field. It keeps moving forward while gravity pulls it downward at the same time. Because these two motions occur together, the ball follows a smooth curved path called a parabola.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals → Trajectory and Independence of Horizontal and Vertical Motion for detailed explanations, worked examples, and additional JAMB-style practice questions in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question:
An agricultural engineer tests an irrigation sprinkler that ejects water at a speed of 30 m s⁻¹ making an angle of 53° with the horizontal. Neglecting air resistance and take sin 53° = 0.8 and cos 53° = 0.6.

Which combination correctly gives the horizontal and vertical components of the initial velocity of the water jet?

options.A: 18 m s⁻¹ horizontally and 24 m s⁻¹ vertically
options.B: 24 m s⁻¹ horizontally and 18 m s⁻¹ vertically
options.C: 30 m s⁻¹ horizontally and 24 m s⁻¹ vertically
options.D: 18 m s⁻¹ horizontally and 30 m s⁻¹ vertically

answer_text: 18 m s⁻¹ horizontally and 24 m s⁻¹ vertically

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret the velocity components of a projectile launched at an angle. You are required to resolve the launch speed into its horizontal and vertical components.
explanation.principle:
When a projectile is launched at an angle to the horizontal, its initial velocity is split into two independent components.

The horizontal component is given by:

vₓ = u cos θ

The vertical component is given by:

vᵧ = u sin θ

where:

• u is the launch speed.
• θ is the angle of projection.
• vₓ is the horizontal component of velocity.
• vᵧ is the vertical component of velocity.

The horizontal component remains constant during motion because gravity has no horizontal effect, while the vertical component changes continuously under the influence of gravity.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Launch speed, u = 30 m s⁻¹
Angle of projection, θ = 53°
cos 53° = 0.6
sin 53° = 0.8

Step 2: Calculate the horizontal component.

vₓ = u cos θ
= 30 × 0.6
= 18 m s⁻¹

Step 3: Calculate the vertical component.

vᵧ = u sin θ
= 30 × 0.8
= 24 m s⁻¹

Step 4: Compare the calculated values with the options.

The correct combination is 18 m s⁻¹ horizontally and 24 m s⁻¹ vertically.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the horizontal and vertical components have been interchanged.
Option C is incorrect because the entire launch speed cannot be the horizontal component when the projectile is launched at an angle.
Option D is incorrect because the vertical component cannot be equal to the total launch speed unless the projectile is projected vertically upward.
explanation.exam_tip:
For any projectile launched at an angle, remember:

• Horizontal component = u cos θ.
• Vertical component = u sin θ.

A common mistake in JAMB is to interchange the sine and cosine functions. Since the angle is measured from the horizontal, always use cosine for the horizontal component and sine for the vertical component.
explanation.final_answer: A. 18 m s⁻¹ horizontally and 24 m s⁻¹ vertically
explanation.simple_explanation:
Think of the water jet as moving in two directions at the same time. One part of its speed carries it forward, while the other part carries it upward.

The forward speed is:
18 m s⁻¹

The upward speed is:
24 m s⁻¹

Together, these components produce the curved path of the projectile.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals → Resolving Velocity into Horizontal and Vertical Components for detailed explanations, worked examples and additional JAMB-style practice questions available in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question:
During a projectile motion investigation in a university Physics laboratory, a steel ball was projected horizontally from the edge of a bench while a motion sensor measured its horizontal position at equal time intervals. The readings showed that the ball covered equal horizontal distances in equal intervals of time until it struck the floor.

Which conclusion is best supported by the experimental observations?

options.A: The horizontal velocity of the ball increased continuously because of gravity.
options.B: The horizontal motion of the ball was uniform because no horizontal force acted on it after projection.
options.C: The vertical motion remained at a constant speed throughout the flight.
options.D: The mass of the steel ball caused its horizontal speed to remain constant.

answer_text: The horizontal motion of the ball was uniform because no horizontal force acted on it after projection.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret the results of a projectile motion experiment. You are required to identify the physical principle that explains why the ball covered equal horizontal distances in equal time intervals.
explanation.principle:
Projectile motion consists of two independent motions occurring simultaneously.

The horizontal motion is uniform because, in ideal projectile motion, no horizontal force acts on the projectile after it is launched. Air resistance is neglected in this analysis.

The vertical motion is different. Gravity acts vertically downward with an approximately constant acceleration of 9.8 m s⁻² (often taken as 10 m s⁻² in JAMB calculations). Therefore, only the vertical component of the motion is accelerated.

A key experimental observation is that equal horizontal distances covered in equal time intervals indicate constant horizontal velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the experimental observation.

The motion sensor recorded equal horizontal distances during equal time intervals.

Step 2: Recall the meaning of this observation.

Equal distances covered in equal times signify uniform motion, which means the horizontal velocity remained constant.

Step 3: Identify the reason.

Since gravity acts only vertically downward, it does not alter the horizontal component of the velocity. Therefore, the horizontal motion remains uniform throughout the flight, provided air resistance is neglected.

Step 4: Select the option that correctly explains the observation.

Option B correctly states both the observation and the physical reason behind it.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because gravity acts vertically downward and does not increase the horizontal velocity of a projectile.
Option C is incorrect because the vertical motion is accelerated by gravity, so the vertical speed changes continuously during the flight.

Option D is incorrect because the constancy of the horizontal speed is not due to the mass of the projectile. In ideal projectile motion, projectiles of different masses behave similarly when air resistance is neglected.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever an experiment shows equal horizontal distances covered in equal time intervals, immediately associate this with constant horizontal velocity. Remember that gravity changes only the vertical motion of a projectile, not its horizontal motion.
explanation.final_answer: B. The horizontal motion of the ball was uniform because no horizontal force acted on it after projection.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine sliding a puck smoothly across a frictionless table while it also begins to fall off the edge. It continues moving forward at the same horizontal speed because nothing is pushing or pulling it sideways. At the same time, gravity pulls it downward, causing only the vertical motion to change.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals → Experimental Investigation to learn how laboratory observations demonstrate the independence of horizontal and vertical motions, with worked JAMB-style examples and practical interpretations.

question:
A motion sensor was used in a Physics laboratory to investigate the horizontal motion of a small ball projected from the edge of a table. The sensor produced the following data:

Time (s):      0     0.5     1.0     1.5
Horizontal distance (m): 0     3.5     7.0     10.5

Neglecting air resistance, which conclusion is best supported by the experimental data?

options.A: The horizontal velocity increases uniformly because the horizontal distance covered becomes larger with time.
options.B: The horizontal velocity remains constant because equal distances are covered in equal time intervals.
options.C: Gravity causes the horizontal velocity to decrease gradually throughout the motion.
options.D: The projectile experiences horizontal acceleration equal to the acceleration due to gravity.

answer_text: The horizontal velocity remains constant because equal distances are covered in equal time intervals.

explanation.question_restate: The question presents experimental data obtained from a motion sensor during a projectile experiment. You are required to interpret the data and determine what it reveals about the horizontal motion of the projectile.
explanation.principle:
Projectile motion consists of two independent motions occurring simultaneously.

The horizontal motion is uniform because, in ideal projectile motion, no horizontal force acts on the projectile after it is launched. Consequently, the horizontal acceleration is zero and the horizontal velocity remains constant.

A useful indicator of constant velocity is that equal distances are covered in equal intervals of time.

The horizontal velocity can be determined using:

vₓ = Horizontal distance ÷ Time
steps:
Step 1: Examine the time intervals.

The readings were taken every 0.5 s.

Step 2: Compare the distances covered during each interval.

From 0 to 0.5 s: 3.5 m

From 0.5 to 1.0 s: 3.5 m

From 1.0 to 1.5 s: 3.5 m

The projectile covers the same horizontal distance during each equal time interval.

Step 3: Calculate the horizontal velocity.

vₓ = 3.5 ÷ 0.5 = 7.0 m s⁻¹

This value is the same for every interval, confirming that the horizontal velocity is constant.

Step 4: Draw the conclusion.

The experimental data support the conclusion that the projectile moves with constant horizontal velocity while gravity acts only in the vertical direction.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because the distances covered in each equal time interval are the same, not increasing. Therefore, the horizontal velocity does not increase.
Option C is incorrect because gravity acts vertically downward and does not reduce the horizontal velocity in ideal projectile motion.
Option D is incorrect because the horizontal acceleration is zero. The acceleration due to gravity acts only vertically.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever experimental data show equal distances covered in equal time intervals, immediately think of constant velocity. In projectile motion, this principle applies to the horizontal component, while gravity changes only the vertical motion.
explanation.final_answer: B. The horizontal velocity remains constant because equal distances are covered in equal time intervals.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine pushing a toy car so that it rolls smoothly across a level floor. If it moves the same distance every second, its speed is constant. Likewise, the projectile in this experiment travels the same horizontal distance every 0.5 s, showing that its horizontal velocity remains constant even though gravity pulls it downward.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals → Experimental Data Interpretation to learn how to interpret motion sensor results, identify constant horizontal velocity, and solve JAMB-style projectile questions involving laboratory data.

question:
Engineers are testing a warehouse automation robot that launches parcels from a platform onto a receiving conveyor. During a review meeting, four engineers make the following statements about the motion of a parcel after it leaves the launcher, assuming air resistance is negligible:

Engineer I: The parcel continues to accelerate horizontally because it was launched with an initial horizontal speed.

Engineer II: The horizontal and vertical motions of the parcel are independent of each other.

Engineer III: The acceleration acting on the parcel is directed vertically downward throughout its flight.

Engineer IV: Increasing the mass of the parcel alone will increase the time it remains in the air.

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question evaluates your understanding of the fundamental principles of projectile motion by asking you to identify which statements correctly describe the motion of a launched parcel.
explanation.principle:
A projectile is an object that moves under the influence of gravity alone after it has been launched, provided air resistance is neglected.

Projectile motion consists of two independent motions occurring simultaneously:

• Horizontal motion, in which the horizontal velocity remains constant because no horizontal force acts on the projectile.

• Vertical motion, in which gravity produces a constant downward acceleration of approximately 9.8 m s⁻² (or 10 m s⁻² for most JAMB calculations).

The mass of the projectile does not affect its motion in ideal projectile motion because gravitational acceleration is the same for all masses.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Engineer I.

The statement is incorrect. An initial horizontal speed does not produce horizontal acceleration. Since no horizontal force acts after launch, the horizontal velocity remains constant.

Step 2: Examine Engineer II.

This statement is correct. The horizontal motion and vertical motion are independent and can be analysed separately.

Step 3: Examine Engineer III.

This statement is correct. Gravity acts vertically downward throughout the flight, giving the projectile a constant downward acceleration.

Step 4: Examine Engineer IV.

This statement is incorrect. In ideal projectile motion, increasing only the mass does not increase the time of flight. The time in the air depends on the vertical component of the launch velocity and gravity.

Step 5: Identify the correct combination.

Only Engineers II and III made correct statements.

Therefore, the correct option is B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because Engineer I is wrong. A projectile does not continue to accelerate horizontally after launch.
Option C is incorrect because it includes Engineer I, whose statement contradicts the principle of constant horizontal velocity.
Option D is incorrect because although Engineers II and III are correct, Engineer IV is wrong since mass alone does not affect the time of flight.
explanation.exam_tip:
When answering JAMB questions on projectile motion, remember these key facts:

• Gravity acts only in the vertical direction.
• Horizontal velocity remains constant when air resistance is neglected.
• Horizontal and vertical motions are independent.
• The mass of a projectile does not influence its motion in ideal conditions.

Many distractors are based on the common misconception that a moving object must continue to be pushed horizontally after it has been launched.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine throwing two balls of different masses with the same initial speed and direction. Ignoring air resistance, both follow the same type of path and remain in the air for the same length of time because gravity acts equally on both. The only acceleration during flight is vertically downward due to gravity.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals for a complete lesson on the independence of horizontal and vertical motion, the effect of gravity, velocity components, common misconceptions, and additional JAMB-standard worked examples.

question:
Engineers testing an autonomous campus delivery robot program it to launch emergency medical supplies from a platform. During one test, the package leaves the launcher with a speed of 26 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 60° to the horizontal. Neglect air resistance and take g = 10 m s⁻².

Which of the following statements correctly describes the motion immediately after the package is launched?

options.A: The horizontal velocity decreases steadily because gravity acts in the direction of motion, while the vertical acceleration is zero.
options.B: The horizontal velocity remains constant, the vertical motion has a constant downward acceleration of 10 m s⁻², and the initial vertical component of velocity is about 22.5 m s⁻¹.
options.C: Both the horizontal and vertical components of velocity remain constant because the launch speed is constant.
options.D: The horizontal and vertical components are each 26 m s⁻¹ because they are both derived from the launch speed.

answer_text: The horizontal velocity remains constant, the vertical motion has a constant downward acceleration of 10 m s⁻², and the initial vertical component of velocity is about 22.5 m s⁻¹.

explanation.question_restate: This question combines the major ideas introduced in projectile motion fundamentals. You are required to evaluate statements about velocity components and acceleration immediately after a projectile is launched.
explanation.principle:
Projectile motion consists of two independent motions occurring simultaneously.

The horizontal motion is uniform because, in the absence of air resistance, no horizontal force acts on the projectile.

The vertical motion is uniformly accelerated because gravity acts vertically downward.

When a projectile is launched with speed u at an angle θ:

Horizontal component:

vₓ = u cos θ

Vertical component:

vᵧ = u sin θ

The acceleration due to gravity affects only the vertical motion.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Launch speed, u = 26 m s⁻¹

Launch angle, θ = 60°

Step 2: Determine the horizontal component.

vₓ = 26 × cos 60°

= 26 × 0.5

= 13 m s⁻¹

This horizontal velocity remains constant throughout the motion because gravity has no horizontal effect.

Step 3: Determine the vertical component.

vᵧ = 26 × sin 60°

= 26 × 0.866

≈ 22.5 m s⁻¹

Step 4: Determine the acceleration.

Immediately after launch, the only acceleration acting on the projectile is the gravitational acceleration.

Therefore,

a = 10 m s⁻² downward.

Step 5: Compare the statements.

Only Option B correctly combines all three facts:

• constant horizontal velocity,
• downward acceleration due to gravity,
• initial vertical component of approximately 22.5 m s⁻¹.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because gravity acts vertically downward, not horizontally. Hence the horizontal velocity does not decrease, and the vertical acceleration is not zero.
Option C is incorrect because only the horizontal velocity remains constant. The vertical velocity changes continuously under gravity.

Option D is incorrect because the velocity components are obtained by resolving the launch velocity using trigonometric ratios. Neither component is equal to the full launch speed unless the launch direction is entirely along one axis.
explanation.exam_tip: In projectile motion, always separate the motion into horizontal and vertical components before analysing it. Remember that gravity changes only the vertical motion, while the horizontal velocity remains constant when air resistance is neglected.
explanation.final_answer: B. The horizontal velocity remains constant, the vertical motion has a constant downward acceleration of 10 m s⁻², and the initial vertical component of velocity is about 22.5 m s⁻¹.
explanation.simple_explanation: Think of the package as moving in two directions at the same time. It keeps moving forward at a constant horizontal speed, while gravity pulls it downward. The upward part of its motion starts at about 22.5 m s⁻¹ but keeps changing because of gravity. Therefore, Option B is correct.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Projectile Motion Fundamentals for a complete lesson on projectile characteristics, velocity components, independence of horizontal and vertical motion, gravitational acceleration, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional concept reviews and examination practice.

question: A delivery drone flying steadily above a community health centre releases a small parcel without giving it any upward or downward push. Which statement correctly describes the motion of the parcel immediately after it leaves the drone?

options.A: It has only a horizontal velocity, while its initial vertical velocity is zero.
options.B: It has only a vertical velocity because gravity acts immediately.
options.C: It has equal horizontal and vertical velocities at the instant of release.
options.D: Its horizontal velocity becomes zero as soon as it leaves the drone.

answer_text: It has only a horizontal velocity, while its initial vertical velocity is zero.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the defining characteristic of horizontal projection by asking what happens to an object at the exact moment it is released from a moving drone.
explanation.principle:
Horizontal projection occurs when an object is launched from an elevated point with an initial velocity that is entirely horizontal. At the instant of projection, the object has no initial vertical velocity because it is not pushed upward or downward.

The horizontal and vertical motions are independent of each other.

Horizontal motion:

x = vₓt

where:
• x = horizontal distance travelled
• vₓ = constant horizontal velocity
• t = time

Vertical motion:

uᵧ = 0

because the object begins falling only after release. Gravity then produces a downward acceleration of g, causing the vertical velocity to increase with time according to:

vᵧ = gt.
steps: Step 1: Identify the type of motion described. The parcel is released without any upward or downward push, so this is horizontal projection.
Step 2: Recall the defining feature of horizontal projection. At the instant of release, the initial vertical velocity is zero.
Step 3: Determine the horizontal motion. The parcel continues moving horizontally with the same horizontal velocity it had at the instant of release because gravity acts vertically, not horizontally.
Step 4: Evaluate the options. Only Option A correctly states that the parcel has horizontal velocity while its initial vertical velocity is zero.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because although gravity begins acting immediately, it causes vertical acceleration, not an initial vertical velocity.
Option C is incorrect because the initial vertical velocity is zero, so it cannot be equal to the horizontal velocity unless the horizontal velocity is also zero, which is not the case.
Option D is incorrect because gravity does not stop or reduce the horizontal velocity in ideal projectile motion where air resistance is neglected.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever JAMB mentions an object leaving a table, conveyor belt, aircraft, drone or cliff without being thrown upward, immediately remember that its initial vertical velocity is zero. This is the key feature that distinguishes horizontal projection from angular projection.
explanation.final_answer: A. It has only a horizontal velocity, while its initial vertical velocity is zero.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine rolling a ball off the edge of a table. As it leaves the table, it is already moving forward, but it has not been pushed downward. Gravity then pulls it downward while it continues moving forward. Therefore, it starts with horizontal velocity only, and its initial vertical velocity is zero.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Horizontal Projection for a complete lesson on the characteristics of horizontal projection, independence of horizontal and vertical motion, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional exam-focused practice.

question: Water flows out through a small hole on the vertical side of an overhead storage tank. Immediately after leaving the hole, which characteristic identifies the motion as horizontal projection?

options.A: The water leaves the hole with only a horizontal velocity while gravity acts vertically downward.
options.B: The water leaves the hole with equal horizontal and upward vertical velocities.
options.C: The water leaves the hole with only a downward vertical velocity and no horizontal velocity.
options.D: The water leaves the hole with a horizontal velocity that steadily increases because of gravity.

answer_text: The water leaves the hole with only a horizontal velocity while gravity acts vertically downward.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to recognise the defining feature of horizontal projection using the familiar example of water flowing from an overhead storage tank.
explanation.principle:
Horizontal projection occurs when an object leaves a point with an initial velocity that is entirely horizontal. At the instant of projection, the initial vertical velocity is zero.

After projection:

• The horizontal velocity remains constant because no horizontal force acts on the object.

• Gravity acts only in the vertical direction, producing a downward acceleration of approximately 9.8 m s⁻² (or 10 m s⁻² in most JAMB calculations).

Thus, the horizontal and vertical motions occur independently of each other.
steps:
Step 1: Recall the meaning of horizontal projection.

A body is horizontally projected when it is launched horizontally from a height without any initial upward or downward velocity.

Step 2: Examine Option A.

This correctly states that the water initially possesses only horizontal velocity, while gravity acts vertically downward immediately after it leaves the hole.

Step 3: Examine Option B.

This is incorrect because the water is not projected upward. Its initial vertical velocity is zero.

Step 4: Examine Option C.

This is incorrect because the water clearly moves away from the tank horizontally as well as downward after projection.

Step 5: Examine Option D.

This is incorrect because gravity affects only the vertical motion. It does not increase the horizontal velocity.

Therefore, Option A is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because a horizontally projected object does not have an initial upward vertical velocity.
Option C is incorrect because the object possesses an initial horizontal velocity immediately after leaving the hole.
Option D is incorrect because gravity changes only the vertical component of motion, not the horizontal velocity.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB presents a horizontally projected object, immediately remember these three facts:

• Initial vertical velocity is zero.
• Horizontal velocity remains constant.
• Gravity acts only in the vertical direction.

These principles form the foundation for solving all horizontal projection problems.
explanation.final_answer: A. The water leaves the hole with only a horizontal velocity while gravity acts vertically downward.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine pushing a small ball straight off the edge of a table. At first, the push gives it only horizontal motion. Gravity then pulls it downward while it continues moving forward. Water leaving a hole in an overhead tank behaves in exactly the same way.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Horizontal Projection to learn about the characteristics of horizontal projection, the independence of horizontal and vertical motions, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional practice and exam-focused lessons.

question: During a Physics practical, a steel ball is rolled horizontally off the edge of a laboratory bench. At the instant the ball leaves the bench, what is its initial vertical velocity?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the initial vertical velocity of an object undergoing horizontal projection.
explanation.principle:
Horizontal projection occurs when an object is launched horizontally from an elevated point.

At the instant of projection, the object possesses only horizontal velocity. It has no upward or downward velocity because no vertical push is given.

Gravity begins to act immediately after the object leaves the point of projection, causing the vertical velocity to increase downward with time.

Thus:

• Initial horizontal velocity, uₓ ≠ 0
• Initial vertical velocity, uᵧ = 0
• Vertical acceleration, g = 9.8 m s⁻² (approximately 10 m s⁻² in many JAMB questions)

A beginner may wonder why the object falls if its initial vertical velocity is zero.

The reason is that gravity provides a constant downward acceleration after the object has been projected. The vertical velocity starts from zero and increases steadily as the object falls.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the type of motion.

The steel ball is projected horizontally from the laboratory bench.

Step 2: Recall the defining characteristic of horizontal projection.

Only the horizontal component of velocity exists at the instant of projection.

Step 3: Determine the initial vertical velocity.

Since there is no initial upward or downward motion, the initial vertical velocity is 0 m s⁻¹.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Option A correctly states that the initial vertical velocity is 0 m s⁻¹.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because 9.8 m s⁻¹ is neither the initial vertical velocity nor a constant vertical speed. It is the approximate value of gravitational acceleration.
Option C is incorrect because the horizontal and vertical components of motion are independent. The initial vertical velocity is not equal to the horizontal velocity.
Option D is incorrect because the object is not projected upward. Therefore, it has no initial upward velocity.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever you encounter a horizontal projection question, immediately write:

uᵧ = 0

This simple fact helps you avoid one of the most common JAMB mistakes. Remember that gravity changes the vertical velocity only after projection, while the horizontal velocity remains constant if air resistance is neglected.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine gently pushing a ball straight across a table so that it leaves the edge. At that instant, you have not pushed it upward or downward—only forward. Therefore, its initial vertical velocity is zero. It begins to move downward only because gravity acts on it after it leaves the table.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Horizontal Projection for a complete lesson on the characteristics of horizontal projection, independence of horizontal and vertical motions, time of flight, horizontal range, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional practice and exam-focused revision.

question: At a coconut processing facility, a ripe coconut accidentally rolls off the edge of a storage platform and leaves the platform horizontally. If the platform is 28 m above the ground, how long does it take the coconut to reach the ground? (Take g = 10 m s⁻².)

explanation.question_restate: This question asks you to determine the time taken for an object projected horizontally from a known height to reach the ground.
explanation.principle:
Horizontal projection is a type of projectile motion in which an object is launched with only horizontal velocity. At the instant of projection, its initial vertical velocity is zero. The horizontal and vertical motions occur simultaneously but are independent of each other.

The time taken to reach the ground depends only on the vertical motion and is not affected by the horizontal speed.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Height, h = 28 m
Acceleration due to gravity, g = 10 m s⁻²
Initial vertical velocity, uᵧ = 0

Step 2: Use the vertical motion equation.

Since the object starts with zero vertical velocity:

h = ½gt²

Step 3: Substitute the known values.

28 = ½ × 10 × t²
28 = 5t²

Step 4: Solve for t.

t² = 28/5 = 5.6

t = √5.6 ≈ 2.37 s

Step 5: Select the nearest option.

The time of fall is approximately 2.4 s.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (1.7 s) is too short and would correspond to a much smaller height.
Option B (2.0 s) results from an incorrect calculation or using the wrong equation.
Option D (2.8 s) is too long and overestimates the time required for a 28 m fall.
explanation.exam_tip: For horizontal projection, always calculate the time of flight from the vertical motion first using h = ½gt². The horizontal speed is not required unless you are asked to determine the horizontal distance travelled.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine dropping a coconut straight down from the same height while another coconut rolls off horizontally at the same instant. Both coconuts hit the ground at the same time because gravity controls the vertical motion, regardless of any horizontal movement.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Horizontal Projection to learn how to calculate time of flight, horizontal distance, impact velocity, and solve JAMB-style worked examples.

question: A package slides off the end of a loading-bay conveyor belt with a constant horizontal speed of 14 m s⁻¹. If it remains in the air for 2.5 s before landing on the ground, what horizontal distance does the package travel before it lands? (Neglect air resistance.)

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the horizontal distance travelled by an object that is projected horizontally from an elevated point using its constant horizontal speed and the time it spends in the air.
explanation.principle:
Horizontal projection occurs when an object leaves a point with only horizontal velocity. Gravity acts only in the vertical direction, so the horizontal velocity remains constant throughout the motion.

For horizontal motion:

x = vₓt

where:
• x = horizontal distance travelled (m)
• vₓ = horizontal velocity (m s⁻¹)
• t = time of flight (s)

Since horizontal and vertical motions are independent, the horizontal distance is obtained by multiplying the constant horizontal speed by the time of flight.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Horizontal speed, vₓ = 14 m s⁻¹
Time of flight, t = 2.5 s

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

x = vₓt

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

x = 14 × 2.5

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

x = 35 m

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options.

35 m corresponds to Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (28 m) is obtained by using an incorrect multiplication or reducing the time of flight.
Option C (42 m) results from using an incorrect value for either the speed or the time.
Option D (49 m) is obtained by applying an incorrect relationship instead of x = vₓt.
explanation.exam_tip: For every horizontal projection problem, first identify whether the question concerns horizontal or vertical motion. If you are given the horizontal speed and the time of flight, use x = vₓt directly. Remember that gravity does not change the horizontal velocity.
explanation.simple_explanation:
The package continues moving sideways at the same speed while it is falling. Therefore, the horizontal distance is simply the horizontal speed multiplied by the time spent in the air.

14 × 2.5 = 35 m.

question: An automated baggage handling system at an airport projects a suitcase horizontally from the end of a conveyor belt. If the suitcase lands 54 m from the edge of the conveyor after remaining in the air for 3.0 s, what was its horizontal speed? (Neglect air resistance.)

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to determine the constant horizontal speed of an object projected horizontally when its horizontal distance and time of flight are known.
explanation.principle:
In horizontal projection, gravity acts only in the vertical direction. Therefore, the horizontal velocity remains constant throughout the motion.

The horizontal distance travelled is given by:

x = vₓt

where:
• x = horizontal distance (m)
• vₓ = horizontal velocity (m s⁻¹)
• t = time of flight (s)

If the horizontal distance and time are known, the horizontal speed is obtained by rearranging the equation:

vₓ = x/t
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Horizontal distance, x = 54 m
Time of flight, t = 3.0 s

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

vₓ = x/t

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

vₓ = 54/3.0

Step 4: Calculate the horizontal speed.

vₓ = 18 m s⁻¹

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options.

18 m s⁻¹ corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (15 m s⁻¹) results from an incorrect division.
Option B (16 m s⁻¹) is obtained through an arithmetic error and does not satisfy x = vₓt.
Option D (20 m s⁻¹) would give a horizontal distance of 60 m in 3.0 s, not 54 m.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a horizontal projection question gives the horizontal distance and time of flight, calculate the horizontal speed using vₓ = x/t. Remember that gravity changes only the vertical motion and does not affect the horizontal velocity.
explanation.simple_explanation:
The suitcase moves sideways at a constant speed while it is falling. Therefore, divide the horizontal distance by the time taken:

54 ÷ 3 = 18 m s⁻¹.

question: During routine operations at a quarry, crushed stones leave the end of a conveyor belt horizontally and fall freely to the ground below. If a stone remains in the air for 2.8 s before striking the ground, what is its vertical velocity just before impact? (Take g = 10 m s⁻² and neglect air resistance.)

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of how to determine the vertical velocity of a horizontally projected object just before it reaches the ground.
explanation.principle:
In horizontal projection, the object has no initial vertical velocity. Therefore, its vertical motion is the same as that of an object falling freely under gravity.

The vertical velocity after time t is given by:

vᵧ = gt

where:
• vᵧ = vertical velocity (m s⁻¹)
• g = acceleration due to gravity (m s⁻²)
• t = time of flight (s)

Notice that the horizontal velocity has no effect on the vertical velocity because the horizontal and vertical motions are independent.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Initial vertical velocity, uᵧ = 0 m s⁻¹
Acceleration due to gravity, g = 10 m s⁻²
Time of flight, t = 2.8 s

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

vᵧ = gt

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

vᵧ = 10 × 2.8

Step 4: Calculate the vertical velocity.

vᵧ = 28 m s⁻¹

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options.

28 m s⁻¹ corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (14 m s⁻¹) is obtained by using only half the correct value of the time or acceleration.
Option B (24 m s⁻¹) results from an incorrect multiplication.
Option D (56 m s⁻¹) is obtained by mistakenly doubling the correct answer.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a body is projected horizontally, remember that its initial vertical velocity is zero. To find the vertical velocity after a given time, simply use vᵧ = gt. Do not include the horizontal speed in this calculation.
explanation.simple_explanation:
The stone starts falling with zero vertical speed, but gravity makes it move faster downward every second.

After 2.8 s:

Vertical velocity = 10 × 2.8 = 28 m s⁻¹.
